coredata.dc:title: Phosphoric acid assisted synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from waste biomass for detection of Cr(VI) in aqueous media
coredata.dc:creator: Goswami, Juri
Rohman, Shahnaz S.
Guha, Ankur K.
Basyach, Purashri
Sonowal, Karanika
Borah, Shashwata Pratim
Saikia, Lakshi
Hazarika, Parasa
coredata.dc:description: A green and facile method for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) have been developed using lignocellulosic biomass (banana stem) and phosphoric acid to construct fluorescent sensor for Cr(VI) detection. The effects of phosphoric acid and temperature on the fluorescence behavior of carbon dots have been explored elaborately. The optical and structural properties of the CDs were determined by different characterization techniques. The prepared CDs display excellent fluorescence properties with intense bright blue emission spectra around 447 nm at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The CDs possessing the highest quantum yield (15.1%) have an immense sensing potential to detect Cr(VI) in linear detection range (10–30 μM) with detection limit of 2.4 μM. Furthermore, the CDs have been applied to quantitatively detect Cr(VI) in real water samples. Theoretical studies based on DFT and TDDFT calculations suggested the detection pathway of Cr(VI) occurs via photoinduced electron transfer (PET) which furthur supports the experimental studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Hydrothermal method
Banana stem
Computational analysis
Fluorometric sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from banana stem

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their deepest appreciation to JIST for financial support and Director, CSIR-NEIST for giving the permission to carry out a part of the research work. PB acknowledges DST, New Delhi for providing the INSPIRE Fellowship, KS acknowledges UGC , New Delhi for SRF and LS is thankful in-house project OLP-2037 for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorbance, excitation and emission peak of CDs 3, (b) PL spectra of carbon dots (CDs 1- CDs 5).
(a) Excitation-dependent behavior of CDs 2, (b) Excitation-independent behavior of CDs 3.
(a) pH effect of CDs, (b) PL stability as a function of time, (c) PL stability after 120 min of UV lamp radiation, (d) Effect of NaCl concentration on PL intensity.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of CDs, (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s (d) P 2p and (e) N 1s regions of CDs.
(a, b, d) TEM images, (c) Particle size distribution, (e) HRTEM image and (f) SAED pattern of CDs.

(a) Change in PL intensity with the Cr(VI) concentrations from 0 μM to 250 μM, (b) Calibration curve in the linear range (10 μM–30 μM) is shown as inset. (c) Selectivity of CDs with various metal ions. (d) Selectivity of CDs with various anions and organic small molecules.
Optimized geometry of CDs showing (a) top view and (b) side view.
HOMO and LUMO of CDs along with their energies in eV.
Schematic representation of charge transfer from CDS in S1 state to Cr(VI) ion.
Experimental conditions for the synthesis of carbon dots.
Comparison of different carbon dots for Cr(VI) detection.
Detection of Cr(VI) in water samples.

head.ce:title: Phosphoric acid assisted synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from waste biomass for detection of Cr(VI) in aqueous media

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jorhat Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green and facile method for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) have been developed using lignocellulosic biomass (banana stem) and phosphoric acid to construct fluorescent sensor for Cr(VI) detection. The effects of phosphoric acid and temperature on the fluorescence behavior of carbon dots have been explored elaborately. The optical and structural properties of the CDs were determined by different characterization techniques. The prepared CDs display excellent fluorescence properties with intense bright blue emission spectra around 447 nm at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The CDs possessing the highest quantum yield (15.1%) have an immense sensing potential to detect Cr(VI) in linear detection range (10–30 μM) with detection limit of 2.4 μM. Furthermore, the CDs have been applied to quantitatively detect Cr(VI) in real water samples. Theoretical studies based on DFT and TDDFT calculations suggested the detection pathway of Cr(VI) occurs via photoinduced electron transfer (PET) which furthur supports the experimental studies.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, a highly promising type of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted immense research attention due to its fascinating optical properties and nanosized morphology [1]. Compared to conventional semiconductor type quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit significant properties such as high stability towards various reaction medium, excellent photostability and biocompatibility with magnificent solubility in aqueous medium [2–8]. Owing to these unique properties, CDs evolve as a superior contender for a broad range of applications including chemical and biological sensors, drug delivery, photocatalyst etc. [9–11]. There is an increasing demand for CDs of high performance; however, a major downfall of CDs is high-cost starting materials and low production yield. Therefore, synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) using non-toxic solvents and cost efficient greener starting materials are the fundamental concern.

In recent times, synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from biomass is of utmost interest as they are natural, non-toxic and inexpensive sustainable carbon source [12–17]. At present, there is a growing trend to produce CDs from lignocellulosic biomass precursors (sugarcane basses, wheat straw, rice husk, saw dust etc.) as it is the largest sustainable source of carbon worldwide [18–20]. Banana stem, a lignocellulosic biomass containing hemicellulose (16–20%), cellulose (43–50%), lignin (12–16%) has signified its utilization as starting precursor for the productions of CDs [21]. To gain both economic and environmental benefits, such bulk carbon precursors have been used for the synthesis of CDs, that could also meet the criteria for waste disposal thereby transforming them into value-added products [22–24]. These fluorescent nanomaterials can be synthesized by numerous methods which include microwave irradiation, hydrothermal method, laser ablation, ultrasonic treatment, solvothermal treatment and chemical oxidation method [25–29]. Among all these outlining methods, hydrothermal synthesis has greater advantages over other existing methods and is widely used, which may be due to its simplicity and formation of CDs with good quantum yield [30].

The environmental contamination via the heavy metal ion Cr (VI) has turned out to be a major concern in the ecosystem, due to its highly toxic and carcinogenic properties [31–34]. Therefore, detection of Cr (VI) in different water sources with high sensitivity and selectivity is garnering much attention among researchers [35–39]. Moreover, Cr (VI) is extremely difficult to detoxify chemically and biologically, so it is easily available in ecosystems, thereby causing serious health issues like liver cirrhosis, skin allergy, hemolytic anemia and so on. Till date, many analytical techniques such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), electrochemical techniques and inductively coupled plasmon resonance strategies have been employed for quantitative detection of heavy metal ions [40–42]. However, these methods suffer from many disadvantages like requirement of professional experts to handle the instrument, pre-sampling preparation and massive procedural cost. Recently, fluorometric sensing method is receiving enormous attention due to its tremendous sensitivity, simplicity, short response time and hence has been turned into a promising analytical method for detecting metal ions in aqueous medium [43–47].

The present work addresses the synthesis of CDs from a lignocellulosic biomass (banana stem) by using phosphoric acid through a simple hydrothermal method, and their utilization as fluorometric sensor in detection of Cr (VI). A blue emitting fluorescent CDs is obtained with both excitation-dependent and excitation-independent behavior by controlling the ratio of phosphoric acid and carbon precursors. The effects of temperature and amount of phosphoric acid on the reaction condition have been extensively studied. The CDs with highest quantum yield have been used for further characterization to study the morphological, optical and structural properties. The change in the fluorescence intensity of CDs on addition of different concentration of Cr(VI) ions have been investigated to calculate the LOD and Stern-Volmer constant. DFT calculations for orbital energies and TD-DFT calculations for excited state were performed to validate the fluorometric sensing mechanism. To demonstrate the practical utility, the CDs were used in sensing of Cr(VI) in tap water, river water and pond water with good recovery percentage. Hence, the CDs synthesized from naturally abundant waste banana stem provide a significant environment appeal.

Banana stem was collected from local areas of Jorhat district, Assam. 88% Phosphoric acid and ethanol were purchased from sigma Aldrich, India. The other reagents such as (FeSO4·7H2O, ZnCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, HgCl2, NaNO3, KCl, Zn (NO3)2, K2Cr2O7, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, FeCl3, Na2SO4, CH3COONa, KNO3, Glucose, Sucrose, Glycine, Leucine, Histidine, Lysine) were purchased either from Merck, India or Sigma-Aldrich, India. The stock solutions of the metal ions, anions and organic small molecules were prepared by dispersing a certain calculated quantity (in mg) in 100 ml distilled water and diluted whenever necessary.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from banana stem

ce:section.ce:para: UV–visible spectra of the CDs were recorded on Analytikjena (model: Specord 210 plus). Room temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CDs solutions was obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer Horiba Instruments Inc. Edison, NJ USA equipped with a 150 W ozone free vertically mounted xenon arc lamp and connected with Fluorescence software. A standard quartz cuvette having a path length of 1.0 cm was used. Life time analysis was done using the same fluorescence spectrophotometer. Morphology and size of the CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a JEOL JEM 2100, transmission electron microscope operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Infrared Fourier transform spectra were noted in an IR Affinity-1 FTIR spectrophotometer equipped with a Shimadzu DRS-8000 working in 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the CDs were performed on a Rigaku X-ray diffractometer (model: ULTIMA IV, Rigaku, Japan) recorded at a scanning rate of 3° min−1 with 2θ value ranging from 5° – 100° where the X-ray source is Cu Kα having wavelength of λ = 1.54056 Å. The surface elements were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCALAB Xi+, Thermo Fisher Scientific Pvt. Ltd., UK).

Carbon dot (CDs) and its Cr(VI) complex were fully optimized without any symmetry constraint at M06-2X/6–311++G** level of theory [48]. Harmonic frequency calculations were also performed at the same level of theory to understand the nature of the stationary states. All the structures were found to be at their local minima with all real values of the Hessian matrix. Electronic structure analysis was performed using natural bonding orbital (NBO) program [49].

Excited state and oscillator strength (f) were calculated using TD-DFT method in benzene medium using polarizable continuum model (PCM) [50]. All these calculations were performed using Gaussian 16 suite of program. Extended charge decomposition analysis in the first excited S1 state was also performed using Multiwfn program code [51,52].

The quantum yield of the as-synthesized CDs was estimated using the reference 0.1 mg/L Quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4. The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated by following equation.
(1)
∅
C D s
=
∅ R
×
F
F R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R 2
where CDs and R represents the carbon dots and reference respectively. The parameters Փ, F, A and ƞ represents the quantum yield (ØR = 0.54 for Quinine sulphate), fluorescence integrated intensities, absorbance values and the refractive index of solvent medium (ƞ is 1.33 for both quinine sulphate and CDs) respectively.

Detection of Cr(VI) by CDs was performed using PL spectroscopic technique. Typically, a series of Cr(VI)solution of different concentrations were added to 500 μl of CDs solution separately and the total volume of the cuvette was maintained to 3 ml by adjusting with double distilled water. The fluorescence spectra of the final solution were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and emission wavelength of 447 nm to prepare the Stern-Volmer plots.

For selectivity studies, effect of various metal ions, anions and organic small molecules on the fluorescence intensity of CDs were examined. The stock solutions of Na+, K+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Cr(VI), Fe3+, glucose, sucrose, CH3COO−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, BO4 −, Glycine, Leucine, Histidine, Lysine, were prepared. Initially, 500 μl of CDs solution were added to an appropriate volume of metal ions, anions and organic small molecule solution and then the PL intensity of the resultant solution was measured.

The practical applicability of the synthesized CDs was investigated by spiking various concentration of Cr(VI) in different water samples collected from local areas of Jorhat city, Assam. As the collected water samples might contain many unwanted impurities, so the water samples were filtered through a membrane filter of 0.45 μm pore size. Standard solutions of Cr(VI) of different concentrations were prepared and spiked with the real samples by using standard addition method. The recovery percentage was evaluated using the same fluorometric technique as discussed above. Moreover, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was evaluated by repeating the experiment up to five times under the same experimental conditions.

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared from a lignocellulosic biomass (banana stem) by applying a suitable hydrothermal method. Banana stem contains hemicellulose (16–20%), cellulose (43–50%) and lignin (12–16%) in good amount and phosphoric acid is the appropriate agent for activation of this lignocellulosic biomass, as it is less hazardous to the environment [53]. Here, addition of phosphoric acid catalyzes the scission/cleavage of aryl ether linkages of lignin groups, thus facilitating dehydration reactions which is the driving mechanism of this chemical activation process. The low pH imposed due to the presence of phosphoric acid promotes the degradation of cellulose and hemicellulose to furfurals intermediates which is polymerized by dehydration reactions to form carbon dots.

Optical properties of CDs were explored by UV–Visible and PL studies. Under different experimental conditions, 5 (five) different types of carbon dots solutions were prepared by varying the concentration of phosphoric acid and temperature. The experimental conditions of these CDs with their quantum yield are tabulated in Table 1. The CDs solution is yellowish brown in normal day light, but it displays bright blue luminescence under the UV light. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CDs show a peak at 280 nm which is attributed to π-π* transition corresponding to the carbon core CC (sp2) (blue line of Fig. 1 a). It indicates the presence of conjugated double bonds and carbonyl groups on the surface of carbon dots. The fluorescence spectra of the carbon dots are shown by the (red and black lines of Fig. 1a) where the maximum excitation and emission wavelength are located at 350 and 447 nm respectively. The PL emission spectra of the carbon dots (CDs1-CDs5) are shown in (Fig. 1b) at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and observed maximum fluorescence intensity at emission wavelength of 447 nm.

The effect of concentration of phosphoric acid in the emission behavior of carbon dots were studied by varying the excitation wavelength from 270 to 370 nm, an excitation-dependent behavior for the carbon dots (CDs2) was obtained (Fig. 2 a). However, no major shift in wavelength (λex in-dependent) for the carbon dots (CDs 3) synthesized with higher concentration of phosphoric acid (Fig. 2b) was observed. The difference in emission behavior may be due to their diversified chemical composition with change in concentration of phosphoric acid. Usually, excitation dependent emission results from the particles due to their different sizes and presence of surface functional groups. Similarly, excitation independent emission is mainly for the uniformity on the surfaces rather than the morphology. On increasing the amount of phosphoric acid, excitation independent emission behavior is exhibited because of the decrease in concentration of surface oxygen. However, decrease in amount of phosphoric acid results in an increase in oxygen rich original surface exhibiting excitation dependent emission behavior. To understand the effect of temperature on the emission behavior, CDs 4 and CDs 5 were synthesized at temperature 140 °C and 160 °C respectively. The CDs 5 synthesized at 160 °C was found to show excitation independent emission as shown in Fig. S1(supplementary information). But for the CDs 4 synthesized at 140 °C, besides the emission peak at 447 nm, another peak at 525 nm has also been observed (Fig. S2, supplementary information). The difference in emission peak positions with varying temperature is probably due to the formation of different discrete states with energy levels between π-π* of the carbon-carbon double bonds in the carbon dots.

The quantum yield (QY) was calculated for each type of CDs and the highest QY was found to be 15.11% for CDs2 which was synthesized at 180 °C temperature and 1:2 ratio for 6 h. On increasing the phosphoric acid concentration, the quantum yield decreased to 5.15%. Moreover, temperature has also a great impact on the quantum yield. The quantum yield at 140 °C was found to be 9.12% whereas it was increased to 13.5% at 160 °C. As the temperature increases, the degradation, carbonization and dehydration routes achieved greater efficiency resulting in higher quantum yield. Similarly negligible concentration of phosphoric acid has a deep impact on the quantum yield of CDs, where CDs1 has very less quantum yield, that is, 0.9%. Therefore, 180 °C and 1:2 ratio is the optimum condition to prepare a high quantum yield carbon dot.

The effect of pH on photoluminescence property of the CDs 2 prepared at 180 °C was investigated. The normal pH of the reaction solution was found to be slightly acidic; however, the pH was adjusted from 4 to 9 by using HCl and Na2CO3. It can be observed that the maximum PL intensity of CDs2 solution was observed at pH 7(Fig. 3 a). The PL intensities are weak at strongly acidic and basic conditions respectively. Thus, pH of the reaction solution has an important effect on the fluorescence intensity owing to the protonation and deprotonation of the different functional groups present on the surface of CDs. Hence, pH 7 was considered as the best choice to provide a high sensing platform. The possible reason for a weak fluorescence at basic pH may be due to PET which occurs because of the lone pairs present in the phosphorous atom of the phosphate groups.

The stability of the carbon dots is an important factor for any fluorometric probe. It was observed that the fluorescent intensity remained stable even after hundred days (Fig. 3b). The fluorescent behavior of the carbon dots was found to be stable for 120 min under continuous exposure of UV lamp irradiation at excitation/emission wavelength (350/447 nm) as demonstrated in Fig. 3c. Similarly, the fluorescence stability of the carbon dots in the presence of different concentration of NaCl solution was studied. From the (Fig. 3d), it is observed that there is no significant change in the fluorescence behavior with varying NaCl concentration, suggesting high PL stability of the CDs under high ionic strength conditions.

XPS analysis was carried out to study the surface composition of the CDs. Although the main components of banana stems are hemicellulose (16–20%) (43–50%) and lignin (12–16%), banana peels also contain a certain amount of tryptophan, vitamin B6 and abundant Serotonin. Therefore, the nitrogen element is also an important part of the prepared carbon dots. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 4 a) shows three significant peaks at 284 eV, 531 eV, 400 eV and 130 eV which can be assigned to C, O, N and P elements with atomic ratios 52.17%, 33.97%, 5.78% and 8.08% respectively. The high-resolution C 1s deconvoluted XPS spectra shows peaks at 284.8, 286.0, 287.2 and 289 eV which are assigned to C–C/CC, C–O, C–O–C and CO groups respectively (Fig. 4b). The high-resolution O 1s deconvoluted spectra of CDs indicates two peaks at 531 eV and 542.8 eV that arises for (OC) and (OC–OH) groups respectively (Fig. 4c). The P (2p) deconvoluted peaks (Fig. 4d) at 133.1 eV and 134 eV can be ascribed to (P–O) and (P–C) bond. The N 1s deconvoluted spectra has been added in Fig. 4e. The N 1s deconvoluted spectra shows fitted peaks at 398.5, 399.0 and 400.6 eV which are assigned to C–N–C, C–N–H and N-(C)3 groups respectively. Thus, the XPS spectra suggest the presence of oxygen (O), carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and acceptable amounts of Phosphorous (P) on the surface of carbon dots. The existence of these functionalities is responsible for their excellent dispersibility in water.

The FTIR analysis was carried out to reveal the functional groups present in the CDs (Fig. 5 ). Distinctive bands including stretching vibrations of OH/NH at 3409.4 cm−1, CO–NH at 1634.7 cm−1, C–H at 2915.5 cm−1 and the strong band at 1131.2 cm−1 due to C–O bond were observed. Also, the band at 1005.9 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibrations of PO groups. From the above analysis, it is observed that the prepared CDs have enough functional groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, amino, phosphate salts and many others which make it highly hydrophilic in nature. Remarkably, majority of the groups on the particle's surface contains the oxygen element, which is well consistent with the XPS analysis displaying high oxygen content.

Size and morphology of the CDs were confirmed by Transmission Electron Microscopy study where well distributed particles of sizes 2–10 nm with quasi-spherical structure could be observed (Fig. 6 a, b, d). The TEM images of various magnifications show the CDs with irregular spherical shape having different sizes, exhibiting excellent dispersibility in aqueous solution. The size distribution of the CDs is presented in the form of a histogram in (Fig. 6c). Further, for analyzing the size distribution nearly 180 particles were scanned using Image J software. The internal structural characterization is done by HRTEM analysis and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm and 0.29 nm which have been ascribed to the (100) and (002) facet of graphitic carbon. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 6f) displays a set of concentric rings with bright spots which suggest the presence of diffraction planes showing the crystalline nature of prepared CDs. The XRD pattern of carbon dots showed a peak at 2θ = 25.5° indicating highly disordered carbon atoms that are assigned to (002) planes of graphitic carbon which clearly indicates the graphitic nature of C-dots (Fig. S4, supplementary information).

Bestowed with the unique properties, the CDs are proposed to serve as potential sensor for detecting Cr(VI), in terms of a decrease in fluorescence intensity (quenching) of CDs depending on the concentrations of Cr(VI). Fig. 7 a shows the plot of PL intensity of the CDs with different concentration (0–250 μM) of Cr(VI)solution. It can be easily seen that, as the concentration of Cr(VI) increases the PL intensity of CDs gradually decreases. The Stern-Volmer calibration curve has been plotted following the equation:
(2)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]
where, I0 and I represents the fluorescence intensities before and after addition of Cr(VI) to the CDs solution. Ksv and Q are the Stern-Volmer constant and concentration of Cr(VI) ions respectively. A calibration curve as shown by (Fig. 7b) was obtained by plotting linear concentration range (10–30 μM) against I0/I value. The linearity of the plot from 10 to 30 μM is quite high having R squared value of 0.997 as shown in (Fig. 7b) with Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv value) 1.1 × 104 M−1. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated on this linear range based equation (3) and was found to be 2.4 μM. Table 2 displays the comparison of detection limits of Cr(VI) with various carbon dots for Cr(VI) detection.
(3)
L O D =
3.3 σ
s l o p e
where, σ is the standard deviation.

To evaluate the selectivity of the CDs towards Cr(VI), the fluorescent quenching was investigated with some other metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Fe3+), anions (CH3COO−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, BO4 −) and organic small molecules (glucose, sucrose, gly, his, leu, lys) at an excitation/emission wavelength of 350/447 nm. The bar diagram (Fig. 7c and d) shows that, only Cr(VI) had the ability to quench the PL intensity of CDs while the other metal ions, anions and organic small molecules had a little or no quenching effects. Thus, the CDs displays an exceptional selectivity for Cr(VI) than other metal ions.

The recovery studies were performed for practical utility of the synthesized CDs in three different water samples i.e., tap water, river water and pond water. Here, 10 and 30 μM Cr(VI) solutions have been used for spiking in the real water samples in the presence of CDs. PL analysis was performed with the CDs and the water samples before and after addition of the standard solutions of Cr(VI) so as to determine the unknown concentration of Cr(VI). Table 3 shows the recovery % and Relative Standard Deviation (RSD) of the water samples. The recovery percentage falls in the range of 97–104% which reveals that the CDs are capable of detecting Cr(VI) in real samples.

Moreover, the optimal temperature for the detection of Cr (VI) was room temperature (298 K). We have performed all the experiments at this temperature. The optimal time for sensitive detection of Cr(VI) have been depicted in Fig. 8 . From Fig. 8, it can be seen that, the fluorescence was quenched immediately within 20 s, while the intensity kept stable up to 120 s. The quick response indicated that CDs 2 offered the possibility to realize favorable simplicity for Cr(VI) sensing.

The literature reports suggest different mechanism for quenching. Different routes like photo induced Electron Transfer (PET), Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), Dark Resonance Energy Transfer (DRET) and Internal Filtration Effect (IFE) are quite common [54–56].

In this study we performed Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations and life time decay analysis to study the mechanism of quenching on interaction of fluorophore and Cr(VI). To understand the life time decay, fluorescence life time experiment has been performed. The life time of fluorescence decay for the CDs was found to 974.73 ps and on addition of quencher the life-time gradually decreases to 721.32 ps (Fig. S3, supplementary information). The lifetime decay experiment in absence and presence of quencher shows that the fluorescence life time gradually decreases on addition of quencher to the fluorophore (CDs) and thus reveals that the quenching is dynamic and to be held at the excited state of the system [57]. Moreover, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs 2, Cr (VI) and CDs 2 + Cr(VI) are shown in Fig. S5. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) showed two characteristic absorption peaks at 257.0 and 349.5 nm. After the addition of the quencher Cr(VI), the UV–vis spectrum of CDs 2 + Cr(VI) mixed solution mostly match the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs 2 and Cr(VI), indicating that there was no new substance to form and the observed quenching effect of CDs could not be ascribed to the static quenching process (Fig. S5, supplementary information). Therefore, the quenching process is dynamic.

For a deep understanding of the mechanism, Time Dependent Density Functional Theory (TDDFT) calculation has been carried out. Fig. 9 shows the optimized geometry of CDs. Fig. 10 shows the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) along with their energies. HOMO represents the delocalized π orbital with energy −3.38 eV while LUMO represents the π* orbital with energy −0.78 eV. We then computed the natural charge by NBO method to get an idea about the possible site of attachment of the Cr(VI) ion. We found that O atom attached to phosphate group and the carbonyl oxygen (O1 and O2) have the most negative natural charge (−0.621 and −0.546 |e|, respectively). We therefore, envisioned that these two O (O1 and O2 in Fig. 9a) atoms are the most probable binding site for the Cr(VI) ion.

After locating the most probable site of binding of the Cr(VI) ion, we optimized the CDs-Cr(VI) complex in both ground S0 and first excited S1 state. The binding energy of Cr(VI) ion to CDs is very high (112.3 kcal/mol). The binding occurs through charge transfer from O1 and O2 to the Cr(VI) ion as evident from NBO analysis. The natural charge at O1 and O2 in the free CDs dramatically decreased upon coordination to Cr(VI) center. The charge at O1 and O2 in the CDs-Cr(VI) complex became −0.112 and −0.093 |e| respectively.

We have also optimized the CDs-Cr(VI) complex in S1 state and calculated the charge transfer using extended charge decomposition analysis. Calculation revealed 0.72 amount electron transfer from CDs to Cr(VI). The electron transfer from CDs is expected to hinder the electron-hole recombination and may result in fluorescence quenching phenomenon. As Cr(VI) has no d-electrons and hence the lowest unoccupied orbital of Cr(VI) will be very low in energy. Electron transfer from CDs to Cr(VI) will lead to significant potential drop and result in fluorescence quenching. The electron transfer mechanism is depicted in (Fig. 11 ). The mechanism of this reaction is referred to as photoinduced electron transfer or PET which is an excited state electron transfer process between the fluorophore and the quencher resulting in decaying fluorescence life time as observed herein (see Fig. 11).

Carbon dots (CD) were synthesized from lignocellulosic biomass of banana stem and phosphoric acid as precursors by cost effective hydrothermal method. The CDs were formed with the average size of 9 nm with stable and strong fluorescence behavior. The CDs acted as a suitable fluorophore for selective detection of toxic metal ion such as Cr(VI) with detection limit (LOD) of 2.4 μM. Here, CDs with different quantum yield have been prepared by varying the ratio of carbon precursor to phosphoric acid and temperature. The experimental study as well as theoretical calculations suggest a PET mechanism for the detection. Further, the sensing probe was corroborated in different real water samples with reasonable recoveries (97–104%). Thus, the utilization of these waste biomass facilitates a green and large-scale production of high-valued CDs, thereby permitting its development and wider sustainable application in the near future.

Juri Goswami: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shahnaz S. Rohman: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ankur K. Guha: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Purashri Basyach: Formal analysis, Validation. Karanika Sonowal: Formal analysis. Lakshi Saikia: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Parasa Hazarika: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their deepest appreciation to JIST for financial support and Director, CSIR-NEIST for giving the permission to carry out a part of the research work. PB acknowledges DST, New Delhi for providing the INSPIRE Fellowship, KS acknowledges UGC, New Delhi for SRF and LS is thankful in-house project OLP-2037 for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2022.126133.
